sources.title: A history of DORA’s software delivery metrics
The new identity of a developer: What changes and what doesn’t in the AI era

items.title: Platform engineering only works when it is user-centric
items.summary: DORA 2024 explicitly ties user-centricity to better product and delivery outcomes, which raises the bar for internal platform UX.
items.implication: Measure platform success with developer task completion, not service inventory growth.

items.title: Self-service quality matters more than catalog breadth
items.summary: A wide platform surface without clear golden paths increases cognitive load and lowers adoption.
items.implication: Simplify paved-road journeys and remove optionality that creates setup hesitation.

kpis: Time from repo creation to first production deployment

items.title: AI changes the developer role toward orchestration and verification
items.summary: GitHub's research points to developers acting more as orchestrators, which increases the value of trustworthy platform guardrails.
items.implication: Design platforms for review, traceability, and policy-aware automation rather than only scaffolding generation.

items.title: Delivery instability needs its own visible metric
items.summary: DORA's five-metric framing separates throughput from instability, making hidden rework visible.
items.implication: Expose deployment rework, recovery time, and change failure trends in platform scorecards.

items.title: Onboarding speed is now a strategic platform KPI
items.summary: Teams adopt platforms when they can ship value quickly; long setup windows signal low leverage.
items.implication: Treat repo-to-prod time and environment provisioning wait as executive metrics.

items.title: Platform standards fail when they remove engineering judgment
items.summary: Senior engineers adopt paved roads only when extension points and exceptions are explicit and safe.
items.implication: Support controlled escape hatches with policy, review, and migration guidance.

items.title: Shared controls should reduce bottlenecks, not centralize them
items.summary: Security and reliability baselines add value only when they scale through automation rather than ticket queues.
items.implication: Push baseline controls into templates, policy engines, and continuous verification.

items.title: Platform leverage is a portfolio question, not a team slogan
items.summary: Specialist platform teams create value when they reuse common foundations instead of rebuilding them.
items.implication: Track shared-foundation reuse and investment avoided across specialist platforms.

kpis: Shared-foundation reuse across specialist platform teams

items.title: Developer satisfaction is a delivery input, not a soft metric
items.summary: DORA and GitHub both reinforce that better experience affects actual engineering output and adoption.
items.implication: Measure friction, confidence, and clarity directly alongside delivery KPIs.

items.title: AI-assisted development increases the need for strong templates
items.summary: As more code and workflows are delegated to AI, template quality becomes a multiplier for both speed and correctness.
items.implication: Invest in opinionated starter kits, reference architectures, and validation pipelines.

items.title: Control coverage should be auditable at service level
items.summary: Platform governance becomes credible only when every production service can prove baseline conformance and exceptions.
items.implication: Render control status per service, with evidence and owner-visible remediation paths.

items.title: Repo creation is not onboarding completion
items.summary: Teams only feel onboarded when they can safely deploy, observe, and operate a service without bespoke help.
items.implication: Design onboarding as a full journey through deploy, runtime visibility, and recovery.

kpis: Time from repo creation to first production deployment

items.title: Stable priorities are part of platform performance
items.summary: DORA highlights stable priorities as a performance factor, which means platform churn can silently hurt delivery teams.
items.implication: Reduce surprise migrations and communicate platform roadmap changes as product changes with migration support.

items.title: Golden paths need runtime observability baked in
items.summary: A service that deploys quickly but lacks standard telemetry still creates support debt and governance gaps.
items.implication: Make logs, traces, alerts, and ownership metadata mandatory platform defaults.

items.title: Specialist platforms win by composing, not forking
items.summary: Data and ML teams move faster when they extend shared identity, deployment, and telemetry foundations.
items.implication: Formalize extension contracts for specialist platforms on top of the common base.

items.title: Platform support demand is an adoption smell
items.summary: If engineers need frequent human help to use the platform, the platform is not truly self-service.
items.implication: Instrument support tickets, failed flows, and abandoned provisioning paths as product analytics.

items.title: AI tooling amplifies weak platform documentation
items.summary: AI agents and copilots produce better results when docs, conventions, and templates are clear and consistent.
items.implication: Treat platform documentation quality as a build input for both humans and agents.

items.title: Platform ROI needs explicit migration economics
items.summary: Leverage claims weaken when teams cannot see what bespoke pipelines or legacy paths cost them.
items.implication: Expose migration payback and ongoing maintenance savings per adopted capability.

items.title: Reliable exceptions are part of a mature platform
items.summary: Teams will always need some deviations; the maturity test is whether those exceptions are governed and reversible.
items.implication: Add exception registration, review cadences, and expiry policies to the platform surface.

items.title: The best platform products reduce both setup time and recovery time
items.summary: Throughput and instability now need to improve together; speed without recovery quality no longer qualifies as platform success.
items.implication: Balance platform roadmaps between faster starts and faster failure recovery.

kpis: Incident MTTR for common platform failure classes
